perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it will hold in strict confidence and not disclose to any third party Confidential Information of the other, except as approved in writing by the other party to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it will endeavor to hold in confidence and not disclose to any third party Confidential Information of the other, except as deemed appropriate by its internal policies.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will hold in strict confidence' to 'will endeavor to hold in confidence' and 'as approved in writing by the other party' to 'as deemed appropriate by its internal policies' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. 'Endeavor' and 'deemed appropriate' are open to interpretation and do not provide a concrete standard for confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The modified language weakens the obligation to a mere 'endeavor' and allows disclosure based on subjective 'internal policies,' which may not meet the standard of 'reasonable efforts' required to maintain trade secret protection under California law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes "will hold in strict confidence" to "will endeavor to hold in confidence" and "as approved in writing by the other party" to "as deemed appropriate by its internal policies." According to the law explanation, this weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The scraped law snippet defines 'improper means' which includes 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the 'strict' obligation to maintain secrecy with the word 'endeavor' and allowing disclosures based on 'internal policies' instead of written approval, the modified contract introduces ambiguity that could be interpreted as not meeting the duty to maintain secrecy. Therefore, the altered text contradicts the need to prevent a breach of duty, thereby creating a legal contradiction under CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Each party will use the Confidential Information for no purpose other than for the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will generally use the Confidential Information for the Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'generally', the modified text allows for unspecified and potentially unauthorized uses of the confidential information, undermining the original restriction to a specific 'Purpose'.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By implying the allowed use of confidential information for something other than the stated purpose, the parties are failing to take action to ensure the trade secret is protected.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The addition of 'generally' in the modified text weakens the obligation to use confidential information solely for the specified 'Purpose,' thus contradicting the requirement to maintain secrecy as per CUTSA, as explained by the LLM. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' as the disclosure or use of a trade secret 'without express or implied consent'. The original text strictly limits use to the 'Purpose', implying consent only for that use. 'Generally' introduces ambiguity, potentially implying consent for other uses, thus undermining the protection of the trade secret and creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of the other party, each party shall promptly return to the other (or destroy if destruction is requested and provide written certification of destruction) all documents and other tangible and intangible materials representing the other’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable written request of the other party, each party shall endeavor to return to the other documents representing the other’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written request' to 'reasonable written request' and removing the obligation to destroy or certify destruction along with changing 'shall promptly return' to 'shall endeavor to return' weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. The 'reasonableness' requirement is vague, and 'endeavor' introduces uncertainty regarding actual compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The change weakens the process to reclaim and destroy the secrets, which contradicts reasonable efforts to ensure secrecy.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially violating CUTSA's requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The original text mandates prompt return or destruction of confidential information upon written request. The changed text replaces 'written request' with 'reasonable written request,' introduces a subjective element, and changes 'shall promptly return' to 'shall endeavor to return,' weakening the commitment. Furthermore, it removes the obligation to destroy or certify destruction. Snippet 1, referencing CIV 3426.1, defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the changed text increases the risk of misappropriation through disclosure or use. This constitutes a breach of the duty to maintain secrecy, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not be reproduced in any form except as reasonably required for the Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information may be reproduced as deemed necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the limitation of 'reasonably required for the Purpose' and replacing it with 'as deemed necessary' grants broad discretion to reproduce confidential information without a defined purpose, significantly increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Broadening reproduction without the strict purpose limits reasonable secrecy.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'reasonably required for the Purpose' with 'as deemed necessary,' broadening the scope for reproducing confidential information. This weakens the obligation to maintain secrecy, potentially violating CUTSA, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain trade secret secrecy. Snippet 1, referencing CIV 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' to include the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who...knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' The modified contract makes it easier to argue that reproduction was 'deemed necessary,' undermining the 'duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.'

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree as recipients of such material nonpublic information, and agree to require any authorized recipient of such material nonpublic information, to not trade directly or indirectly in the other party’s securities while in possession of such material nonpublic information of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that as recipients of such material nonpublic information, they should generally discourage any authorized recipient of such material nonpublic information from trading directly or indirectly in the other party’s securities.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agree' to 'acknowledge,' 'agree to require' to 'should generally discourage', and removing 'while in possession of such material nonpublic information of the other party' weakens the obligation to prevent insider trading. 'Should generally discourage' is far less forceful than an explicit agreement to prevent trading and also is subject to interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The weakened language undermines the duty to prevent insider trading based on material nonpublic information, making it more difficult to enforce compliance with insider trading laws.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation regarding insider trading, creating a contradiction. The original text states the parties 'agree to require' recipients of nonpublic information to not trade, while the changed text says they 'should generally discourage' such trading. This weakens the enforceability of preventing insider trading. The Law Explanation states that Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibit manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities, and the weakened language undermines the duty to prevent insider trading. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states 'It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio'. The modified contract's language significantly reduces the power to enforce the law described in snippet 1 and the Law Explanation.
